nativeRange.habitat: Coral-rich areas of lagoons and seaward reefs; generally found at depths of 0–51 m

decorPreferences: live rock with caves and crevices

diet.notes: Has been documented feeding mainly on fish and crustaceans in the wild. In captivity, has been known to accept meaty foods such as frozen silversides, krill, shrimp, squid, and crustacean flesh. Two to three substantial feedings per week are generally advised. Poor eyesight makes this species prone to biting anything that moves near its mouth — hand-feeding is not recommended.

avoidWith: invertebrates that fit in its mouth

summary: A large, spotted Indo-Pacific moray eel reaching up to 120 cm; generally considered semi-aggressive, primarily nocturnal, and best kept singly in a large fish-only system with secure rock caves and a tightly sealed lid.
careNotes:
Gymnothorax meleagris is distributed across the Indo-Pacific from East Africa to the Hawaiian Islands, Marquesas, and Mangaréva, south to Lord Howe Island, and has also been recorded off South Africa in the Southeast Atlantic. It tends to inhabit coral-rich lagoons and seaward reefs at depths of 0–51 m and is commonly reported hunting during low tide on partly exposed reefs.

This species is primarily nocturnal — it typically hides in rock caves and crevices during the day and becomes active at night. Aquarists may need to adjust feeding schedules to accommodate this nighttime activity pattern.

A secure, tightly fitting tank cover with no gaps is critical; moray eels of this genus are notorious escape artists and can push through surprisingly small openings. A weighted or latched lid is generally advised.

Poor eyesight combined with powerful jaws and sharp teeth makes hand-feeding hazardous. Using a feeding stick or tongs is strongly recommended. The species has been documented reacting to movement and scent rather than visual identification of food items, which has resulted in keeper injuries during hand feeding.

Minimum tank size is commonly quoted at 125 US gallons (approximately 473 L) by retail hobby sources, which broadly reflects the lower bound for a specimen in the 60–90 cm range. Given the species' documented maximum size of 120 cm (FishBase), a larger system of 500 L or more is generally advisable for a full-grown adult. A long tank footprint is preferred over a tall one to allow the eel to move and explore.

Strong biological and mechanical filtration is generally advised, as this species is a messy feeder.

Temperature range shows some disagreement between sources: LiveAquaria recommends 72–78°F (22.2–25.6°C), while FishBase modeled data indicates 24.5–29°C. The values used here (23–27°C) represent an average across the two sources. Aquarists should note that the FishBase range better reflects wild tropical Indo-Pacific conditions, and maintaining the upper half of the range (25–27°C) is broadly consistent with standard reef aquarium practice.

This species is available in the aquarium trade and is sold under several common names including "Turkey Moray" and "Guinea Fowl Moray." The name "White Mouth Comet Eel" is the trade name commonly used by US saltwater retailers. The species is characterised by a white-and-brown spotted pattern covering the body and fins, and a distinctive white mouth interior.
breedingNotes: Breeding has not been documented in captivity. The species is oviparous in the wild. No captive breeding protocols are available in the aquarium literature.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked scientific classification (family Muraenidae, order Anguilliformes), adult size (120 cm TL max, 80 cm common), native range (Indo-Pacific + Southeast Atlantic), temperature range (24.5–29°C), depth (0–51 m), habitat (coral-rich lagoon and seaward reefs), and diet (mainly fish and crustaceans). Temperature range differs from LiveAquaria (22.2–25.6°C from 72–78°F); averaged across both sources to produce final values of 23–27°C.

fish.breedingNotes: Not yet achieved in captivity. Oviparous in the wild.
